questions.question: Why do activations, not weights, blow up training memory?
options: Activations are stored in fp64

Backprop retains the inputs to every differentiated op — roughly 12·B·L·d floats per layer; at d=8192, L=8192 that's ~800 MB/layer in BF16, 51 GB for 64 layers, before the quadratic attention-softmax intermediates are even counted
Optimizer states live on disk
Weights are always larger
questions.explanation: BF16 weights plus optimizer state might fit in 80GB; activations are what push past it. Checkpointing trades FLOPs for memory: recompute instead of save.

questions.question: How does naive full checkpointing work, and what does it cost?
options: It compresses activations losslessly

Split the network into segments, store only each segment's INPUT, and rerun the segment's forward during backward to rematerialize intermediates — about 33% extra forward FLOPs (a step becomes 1+1+2 = 4 units instead of 3)
It skips backward for some layers
It moves activations to swap space
questions.explanation: Chen et al. 2016: one checkpoint every √L layers balances memory against compute — 8 checkpoints for a 64-layer model.

questions.question: What insight powers selective checkpointing (Korthikanti 2022)?
options: All activations cost the same to store

The attention softmax is B·L²·heads and grows QUADRATICALLY with sequence length while FFN activations grow linearly — keep the cheap linear/residual activations and recompute only attention: ~5x memory saved for under 5% FLOP overhead
Checkpointing works best on embeddings
Recomputing the FFN is free
questions.explanation: Versus naive checkpointing's 33% overhead, the smart selection targets exactly the O(L²) term. Megatron-Core ships it as "selective" recomputation, used in most 2024+ frontier runs.

questions.question: When does offloading beat recomputation?
options: Never — recompute always wins

When idle CPU-GPU transfer bandwidth exceeds the cost of rematerialization: ship activations to CPU RAM between forward and backward — FSDP2 ships it first-class, and mixed strategies (checkpoint some layers, offload others) are common
Only on machines without CPUs
When the model has under 1B params
questions.explanation: The trade is FLOPs vs PCIe bandwidth. The right mix depends on which resource your training run has spare.

questions.question: What exactly does the backward pass need from the forward?
options: Only the final loss value

The INPUT to each op (grad_params = inputᵀ @ grad_output for linears) and activation-derivative intermediates (ReLU/GELU/softmax derivatives depend on activation values) — that's what autograd retains, and what checkpointing chooses to drop and rebuild
All gradients from the previous step
The random seed only
questions.explanation: Knowing what backward consumes is what makes "which segments to drop" answerable — the answer is not "all of them," it's "the expensive-to-store, cheap-to-recompute ones."
